comments.body: Which is basically what I would expect to happen with a scoped prototype bean. Why is it lazy to start with?

Why do you think you need to cache a prototype bean? It is behaving exactly as it was designed You should first explain what you are trying to achieve rather than looking for a solution you think is the correct one. Classical xyproblem.info

@M.Deinum - it's lazy because if it is not lazy then in certain cases (legitimate ones) it can cause a deadlock (cache bean expects other beans to be injected in its configuration). If it wasn't lazy Spring would inject this prototype bean once into singleton and call it a day, which is kinda behaviour I'm looking for (except for a lazy part).

@JorgeCampos I need to cache it because creating a new bean in my case is expensive operation. I want it injected only once into other beans (once that NullController from the example gets created), not on each method call. Simply, I have a prototype bean that I want to instantiate once per injection point, not each time some method is being called on it. And it has to be lazy as otherwise deadlock can happen.
So why not a simple bean (Singleton) with @Lazy (annotation) then? Or Request scoped?

Feels like you are trying to fix a lifecycle problem in the wrong way. If the beans are needed in the configuration then that should be made explicit so Spring knows beforehand what to create when. Ideally you do this by putting the dependencies into the method signatures and not as autowired fields.
@JorgeCampos - because bean isn't a singleton - there will be many different caches. I can not use request scope as caches can be used in non-web apps.
Read the documentation please. docs.spring.io/spring-framework/reference/core/beans/… the default scope of a bean on Spring is Singleton

title: Force Spring to cache lazy prototype beans injected into singleton beans
body:
Is there a way to force Spring to cache prototype beans in LazyDependencyTargetSource? As far as I know, it only caches singletons.

I have a lazy prototype bean, that I want to inject into a singleton bean.

Spring injects a proxy (that is fine), but the proxy creates a new target bean on each access. Normally, when a prototype bean is injected into a singleton bean, this happens only once.

However, with the lazy proxy, the relatively expensive creation of the prototype bean occurs multiple times even for successive method calls.

Custom annotation:

@Target({ElementType.FIELD, ElementType.PARAMETER, ElementType.ANNOTATION_TYPE})
@Retention(RetentionPolicy.RUNTIME)
@Documented
@Value("#{" + "getCache" + "}") // there are other reasons why this is necessary
@Lazy
public @interface CacheAnnotation {
    String value() default "";
}

Creating a new bean is far more complex in my case.

@Configuration
public class CacheConfiguration {
    @Bean
    @Primary
    @Scope(BeanDefinition.SCOPE_PROTOTYPE)
    public Cache getCache() {
        System.out.println("Creating a new bean...");
        return new Cache();
    }
}

Controller:

@RestController
@RequestMapping(path = "/api/cache")
public class NullController {
    @CacheAnnotation("something")
    private Cache cache;

    @GetMapping
    public String getCache() {
        cache.toString();
        cache.toString();
        cache.toString();
        cache.toString();
        cache.toString();
        return "";
    }
}

Hitting this REST endpoint prints

Creating a new bean...

five times.

To work around this, I created a custom BeanPostProcessor that injects a caching proxy into fields and methods. I also had to create a BeanFactoryPostProcessor to enable caching proxy injection into constructors, but there are many moving parts involved. I would prefer to fix the original issue in a simpler way, if possible.
